event: Christopher Columbus sighted the two smaller islands (Cayman Brac and Little Cayman) on his fourth voyage and named them Las Tortugas for the abundant sea turtles.
The islands appeared on a Turin map under the name Lagartos, referring to the caimans or large marine reptiles found there.
The name Caymanas, derived from a Carib word for marine crocodile, came into use and eventually gave the islands their modern name.
English privateer Sir Francis Drake visited the islands and reported the crocodiles known locally as caymanas.
England captured Jamaica from Spain, bringing the nearby uninhabited Cayman Islands under English influence.
The Treaty of Madrid formally ceded the Cayman Islands, along with Jamaica, from Spain to England.
Turtle hunters, privateers, shipwrecked sailors, and deserters used the islands as a base, though no permanent settlement yet took hold.
The first recorded permanent land grant was issued on Grand Cayman, marking the beginning of continuous settlement.
Settler Isaac Bodden, grandson of one of the islands' earliest inhabitants, was born, reflecting the establishment of founding families.
An official survey recorded a small population on Grand Cayman, including enslaved Africans brought to work the land.
Settlers from the Cayman Islands lived primarily by turtling, fishing, woodcutting, and subsistence farming.
The Wreck of the Ten Sail saw ten merchant ships founder on East End reefs, an event that became central to Cayman folklore.
A census recorded a population of 933 people on Grand Cayman, more than half of whom were enslaved Africans.
Caymanians established a local legislative assembly at Pedro St. James, an early step toward self-government.
The abolition of slavery throughout the British Empire freed roughly 950 enslaved people in the Cayman Islands.
A British Act of Parliament formally made the Cayman Islands a dependency of the colony of Jamaica.
Britain appointed the first official Commissioner, Frederick Sanguinetti, to administer the islands directly.
The economy continued to rely on turtling, shipbuilding, and rope-making, with many men serving as seafarers abroad.
A devastating hurricane struck the Cayman Islands, killing more than a hundred people, many of them on Cayman Brac.
Cayman seamen increasingly joined merchant fleets worldwide, with remittances becoming a vital part of the islands' income.
When the West Indies Federation formed, the Cayman Islands remained a dependency of Jamaica but received a new constitution.
When Jamaica became independent, the Cayman Islands chose to remain a British Crown colony rather than join the new nation.
Legislation laid the foundation for the offshore financial industry, establishing the islands as a tax-neutral banking center.
A new constitution introduced the office of Governor and an Executive Council, strengthening internal self-government.
A revised constitution expanded the Legislative Assembly and the role of elected members in government.
The Cayman Islands established a formal banking and trust regulatory framework that accelerated growth as a financial hub.
Tourism boomed as Grand Cayman's Seven Mile Beach and world-class diving drew large numbers of visitors.
Constitutional reforms increased ministerial responsibilities and clarified the division of powers in government.
The Cayman Islands National Museum opened in George Town to preserve and present the islands' maritime and cultural heritage.
The Cayman Islands grew into one of the world's leading offshore financial centers and hedge-fund domiciles.
Hurricane Michelle caused significant flooding and damage across the islands.
Hurricane Ivan struck Grand Cayman directly, causing catastrophic destruction and widespread flooding across the island.
A new constitution took effect, creating a Bill of Rights, a Premier, and a Cabinet while keeping British sovereignty.
McKeeva Bush became the first Premier of the Cayman Islands under the new constitutional arrangement.
Alden McLaughlin became Premier, leading a government focused on financial-services reform and economic recovery.
The Cayman Islands enacted a beneficial-ownership register following international pressure for financial transparency.
The COVID-19 pandemic prompted strict border closures that severely curtailed the tourism-dependent economy.
Wayne Panton became Premier after elections, leading a coalition government known as the PACT administration.
Following general elections, Juliana O'Connor-Connolly became the first female Premier of the Cayman Islands.
André Ebanks became Premier after the general election, leading the newly formed Cayman Community Party government.
